lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Graft-versus-tumor: the silver lining
The same alloreactive donor T cells that cause GVHD also kill residual cancer cells in the recipient.
This is the graft-versus-tumor effect, or GVT, and it is the central therapeutic advantage of allogeneic transplant over autologous.
Here's the evidence.
In the 1980s, transplant oncologists noticed that patients who developed mild-to-moderate GVHD after allogeneic transplant for leukemia had lower relapse rates than patients with no GVHD.
Conversely, when T cells were depleted from donor grafts to reduce GVHD, leukemia relapse rates went up.
The conclusion was inescapable: the same donor T cells that attacked the recipient's normal tissues were also attacking and eliminating residual leukemic cells.
The immune response was, in a sense, the therapy.
This led to a remarkable clinical maneuver called donor lymphocyte infusion, or DLI.
If an allogeneic transplant patient relapses after engraftment, you can collect additional T cells from the original donor and infuse them into the patient.
The donor T cells, recognizing the recipient's leukemia as foreign, attack and often eliminate it.
For chronic myeloid leukemia in particular, DLI alone — without further conditioning, without further transplant — can induce molecular remissions in relapsed patients.
This is one of the most striking demonstrations of the power of allogeneic immunity in oncology and was, in many ways, the conceptual ancestor of all modern cellular immunotherapy.
So the central tension of allogeneic transplant becomes clear.
GVHD and GVT are mediated by the same cells.
You can't fully suppress GVHD without losing GVT.
You can't maximally enhance GVT without unleashing GVHD.
The clinical art is balancing the two.
Modern approaches try to separate them.
Selective T-cell depletion strategies remove the most alloreactive T cells while preserving anti-tumor T cells.
Adoptive transfer of specific virus-specific or tumor-specific donor T cells delivers antitumor benefit without broad alloreactivity.

And — closing the conceptual loop — CAR-T therapy can be thought of as an attempt to deliver pure GVT, targeted at specific tumor antigens, without the GVHD that comes from a polyclonal allogeneic immune response.
GVT is one of the deepest reasons allogeneic HSCT remains the standard of care for many high-risk leukemias even in an era of targeted therapies.
Nothing else delivers durable immune surveillance of residual disease the way a fully engrafted donor immune system does.
